CSCE 450 Syllabus

Section 500 (54772)

Computer Animation

Spring 2026 - College Station

Course Information

Meeting Times:  Meeting Type: LEC

Meeting Days: TR

Start Time: 12:45PM

End Time: 2:00PM

Start Date: 01/12/2026

End Date: 05/05/2026

Meeting Location:  HRBB 113

Credit Hours:  3

Instructor Details

Shinjiro Sueda

Email:  sueda@tamu.edu

Office Location:  PETR 405

Phone:  979-845-4893

Office Hours

TBD

Catalog Description

Computer Animation. (3-0). Credit 3. Investigation of computational problems in

computer animation; study of the mathematical and algorithmic foundations

behind various techniques used for computer animation for real-time and offline

use. Prerequisite: Grade of C or better in CSCE 315 or CSCE 331; grade of C or

better in or concurrent enrollment in CSCE 441.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  Grade of C or better in CSCE 315 or CSCE 331; grade

of C or better in or concurrent enrollment in CSCE 441.

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

Make use of 3D rotations including quaternions

Apply key-frame interpolation of positions and orientations

Choose which spline curves to use for different applications based on

continuity requirements

Compute curve tangents and arc-length parameterization

Apply regression and curve fitting

Use Gaussian quadratures for definite integrals

Implement linear blend skinning

Compare and contrast linear blend skinning and dual quaternion skinning

Apply bilinear/trilinear interpolation for 2D/3D mesh deformation

Apply blend shapes for 3D facial animation

Implement a version of Simple Multi-Person Linear model

Implement gradient descent, Newton’s Method, and L-BFGS with backtracking

line search

Solve 2D inverse kinematics

Compare path-finding algorithms

Apply numerical integration schemes for ordinary differential equations

Explain the relationship between Position-Based Dynamics and Implicit Euler

Integration

Develop a cloth simulation system with collisions

Implement gradient descent, Newton’s Method, and L-BFGS with backtracking

line search

Solve 2D inverse kinematics

Compare path-finding algorithms

Apply numerical integration schemes for ordinary differential equations

Explain the relationship between Position-Based Dynamics and Implicit Euler

Integration

Develop a cloth simulation system with collisions

Special Course Designation

None

Textbook and/or Resource Materials

This material Is: Optional

There is no textbook, but we will make use of several sources for course material.

Besides notes from lectures, we will use several articles and notes available online.

Grading Policy

Final course grades will be based on the following scale:

A = 90% and above

B = 80%-89.999%

C = 70%-79.999%

D = 60%-69.999%

F = <60%

All the milestones for the final project must be met to pass the course.

The grade will be based on participation and programming assignments.

[45%] Five programming assignments

Assignment #1: Keyframed Animation with splines and quaternions

Assignment #2: Skinned Animation

Assignment #3: Skinned Multi-Person Linear Model

Assignment #4: Inverse Kinematics

Assignment #5: Cloth Simulation

[15%] An open-ended final programming project

Mandatory milestones:

proposal

one-on-one meeting

midway report

final presentation

peer report

final webpage

[40%] Two midterms

Non-cumulative

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

"No-Doc" Days

The Allowance : You have a total of 6 "no documentation check" (no-doc) days for

the semester. These can be applied to any programming assignment.

Automatic Application : You do not need to request an extension. These days are

automatically applied to late submissions.

Calculation : Assignments are due at 23:59. Late time is calculated in full-day

increments. (E.g., submitting 1 second past the deadline consumes 1 full no-doc

day).

The Catch : These are not "free" days; they are a buffer for undocumented issues.

If you require more than 6 days total, you must provide documentation for ALL late

days used (days 1–6), not just the days exceeding the limit.

Project

Presentations

AI Statement

In principle you may submit AI-generated code, or code that is based on or derived

from AI-generated code, as long as this use is properly documented in the

comments: you need to include the prompt and the significant parts of the

response. AI tools may help you avoid syntax errors, but there is no guarantee that

the generated code is correct. It is your responsibility to identify errors in program

logic through comprehensive, documented testing. Moreover, generated code,

even if syntactically correct, may have significant scope for improvement, in

particular regarding separation of concerns and avoiding repetitions. The

submission itself must meet our standards of attribution and validation. (From Boris

Steipe (2023) “Syllabus Resources”. The Sentient Syllabus Project

http://sentientsyllabus.org .)
